entries.description_pt_BR: Sistema de documentaĂ§ĂŁo do HERD: 4 artefatos, 6 nĂ­veis, doc-first.
entries.description_en_US: HERD's documentation system: 4 artifacts, 6 levels, doc-first.
entries.body_pt_BR:
--- title: Handbook description: Sistema de documentaĂ§ĂŁo do HERD â€” 4 artefatos, 6 nĂ­veis, doc-first. locale: pt-BR uid: herd.foundation.handbook --- > **Para agentes de IA:** Este arquivo Markdown Ă© a forma canĂ´nica desta entry. Use `Accept: text/markdown` ou adicione `.md` Ă  URL para evitar renderizaĂ§ĂŁo HTML. # Handbook O Handbook Ă© o sistema de documentaĂ§ĂŁo do HERD. Toda feature da plataforma â€” block, tool, foundation ou integration â€” Ă© descrita aqui usando um template consistente, para que humanos, agentes de IA internos (Claude Code) e agentes de IA externos (ChatGPT, Claude Desktop via MCP) consigam construir um modelo mental correto do HERD sem precisar ler cĂłdigo-fonte. Esta entry documenta o prĂłprio Handbook: o que cada level significa, como o `feature.yml` funciona, como ler ou escrever uma entry do Handbook, e como o sistema se mantĂ©m consistente sob mudanĂ§a. ## Business O Handbook existe porque a superfĂ­cie de produto do HERD Ă© grande e estĂˇ em crescimento. Conforme blocks, tools, foundations e integrations se multiplicam, saber o que cada um Ă©, por que existe, a quem serve, e como se relaciona com os outros vira o gargalo central de onboarding â€” tanto para humanos entrando no time quanto para agentes pedidos a fazer trabalho no codebase. O custo de documentaĂ§ĂŁo ruim em um codebase colaborado por IA Ă© materialmente mais alto que em um tradicional. Quando um agente nĂŁo sabe o que uma feature Ă©, ele nĂŁo pergunta â€” ele chuta. Chutes produzem cĂłdigo que compila, roda, e silenciosamente faz a coisa errada. O Handbook elimina as condiĂ§Ăµes sob as quais um agente chuta, fornecendo uma descriĂ§ĂŁo Ăşnica, canĂ´nica, e legĂ­vel por mĂˇquina de toda feature que ele possa tocar. Para os clientes do HERD, o Handbook Ă© invisĂ­vel â€” mas seus efeitos nĂŁo sĂŁo. Entrega de features mais rĂˇpida e consistente; menos regressĂµes causadas por semĂ˘ntica mal entendida; workflows guiados por agentes (via MCP) que funcionam porque o agente tem acesso Ă  mesma documentaĂ§ĂŁo que um engenheiro sĂŞnior consultaria. O Handbook tambĂ©m Ă© o substrato para o posicionamento do HERD como Market Network Platform. Quando a plataforma federar entre corporate networks, o Handbook Ă© o contrato que permite agentes em uma network descobrirem e raciocinarem sobre capacidades em outra sem trabalho de integraĂ§ĂŁo ad-hoc. ## Product O Handbook aparece em trĂŞs lugares. Para humanos no time, vive em `/admin/handbook` dentro do prĂłprio HERD, com uma sidebar Ă  esquerda agrupando entries por level (Foundations, Blocks, Tools, Solutions, Integrations, Corporate Network), e um sumĂˇrio pegajoso em cada entry mostrando as seis perspectives. A renderizaĂ§ĂŁo da UI Ă© entregue em uma etapa posterior; o que existe hoje Ă© a fonte no filesystem. Para o Claude Code trabalhando no repo, vive em `docs/handbook/{level}/{feature-id}/{pt-BR,en-US}.md` â€” lido diretamente do filesystem, pareado com os pacotes `SKILL.md` relevantes em `.agents/skills/`. Para agentes externos (ChatGPT, Claude Desktop) conectando via MCP, aparece atravĂ©s de duas tools: `search(query)` retorna UIDs de features que casam; `fetch(id)` retorna o conteĂşdo Markdown completo de uma entry junto com seu graph de metadata (consumes, consumed_by, related). Um usuĂˇrio lendo o Handbook no admin do HERD vĂŞ: o tĂ­tulo da entry, badge de status (active / draft / deprecated / archived / deferred), versĂŁo, data da Ăşltima atualizaĂ§ĂŁo, e as seis perspectives como seĂ§Ăµes colapsĂˇveis. Cross-references renderizam como links clicĂˇveis para outras entries. ## Architecture ### The 6 commercial levels O produto do HERD Ă© organizado como uma pirĂ˘mide de seis commercial levels. Toda feature documentĂˇvel no HERD pertence a exatamente um level. Os levels, do topo para a base, sĂŁo: ```mermaid flowchart TD CN[Corporate Network] --> SOL[Solution] SOL --> TOOL[Tool] TOOL --> BLOCK[Block] BLOCK --> FDN[Foundation] FDN --> INT[Integration] style CN fill:#1e40af,color:#fff style SOL fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff style TOOL fill:#60a5fa,color:#000 style BLOCK fill:#93c5fd,color:#000 style FDN fill:#bfdbfe,color:#000 style INT fill:#dbeafe,color:#000 ``` - **Corporate Network** â€” a plataforma HERD inteira como aparece para uma Ăşnica empresa cliente. O topo. (Quando a camada Market Network chegar, ela vai sentar acima deste.) - **Solution** â€” um bundle curado de tools vendido ou enquadrado para um resultado de negĂłcio especĂ­fico. Exemplos: Support, Pre-sales, Sales, Marketing. *Atualmente deferred â€” o schema aceita o level mas nenhuma entry existe no day-1; volta quando a camada Solution for desenhada.* - **Tool** â€” uma composiĂ§ĂŁo cross-block com um objetivo de negĂłcio especĂ­fico. Uma tool lĂŞ e escreve em mĂşltiplos blocks, aplica lĂłgica de negĂłcio, e expĂµe uma capacidade focada para usuĂˇrio ou agente. Exemplo: `subscription-offering` (consome contacts + deals + products para gerenciar receita recorrente). - **Block** â€” uma fonte Ăşnica da verdade para um tipo de dado. Um block tem seus prĂłprios Prisma models, endpoints CRUD, ciclo de vida, polĂ­ticas de RLS, e (geralmente) um manifest em `src/lib/blocks/blocks/{name}.block.ts`. Exemplos: `contacts`, `meetings`, `deals`, `products`. - **Foundation** â€” infraestrutura compartilhada que dĂˇ suporte aos levels acima. Foundations nĂŁo tĂŞm unidade comercial por si sĂł; sĂŁo prĂ©-condiĂ§Ăµes necessĂˇrias para blocks, tools, e solutions funcionarem. Exemplos: `i18n`, `domain-events`, `auth`, `permissions`, `audit`, `ledger`, `handbook` (esta entry), `knowledge`, `agents`, `routines`. - **Integration** â€” uma conexĂŁo a um sistema externo. Integrations nĂŁo tĂŞm dado prĂłprio; sĂŁo pontes. Exemplos: `google-calendar`, `slack`, `stripe`. ### The 3 technical categories Cortando os commercial levels, o cĂłdigo do HERD se divide em trĂŞs technical categories usadas no campo `feature.yml.technical_category`: - `block` â€” possui seu prĂłprio dado (Prisma models, CRUD). - `tool` â€” compĂµe dado possuĂ­do por blocks. - `foundation` â€” infraestrutura compartilhada consumida por todos. Nota: `block-group` **nĂŁo** Ă© um level e **nĂŁo** Ă© uma technical category. Block-groups sĂŁo agrupamentos intra-block â€” por exemplo, "packages" como um grupo curado de products dentro do block products, ou um conjunto curado de meetings filtrado por algum critĂ©rio. SĂŁo documentados dentro da Architecture perspective do block pai (no campo `block_groups` do `feature.yml` do pai), nĂŁo como entries separadas. Nota: `category` (Finances, Legal, Marketing, Sales, Operations) **nĂŁo** Ă© um level. Categories sĂŁo agrupamentos de runtime que o orchestrator usa para rotear chamadas de tool. TĂŞm agentes em `.agents/tools/{category}/AGENT.md` mas nĂŁo tĂŞm entries de Handbook â€” o papel comercial que elas teriam Ă© assumido por Solution. ### Decision tree: classifying a new feature Ao introduzir uma nova feature no HERD, percorra a Ăˇrvore abaixo para classificĂˇ-la. O level determina o caminho do diretĂłrio, os artifacts requeridos, e a audiĂŞncia que vai consumir a entry. ```mermaid flowchart TD start{Does it own its own
Prisma data?} start -->|yes| block[level: block] start -->|no| owns_external{Does it connect
to an external
system?} owns_external -->|yes| integration[level: integration] owns_external -->|no| user_facing{Is it user-
facing as a
capability?} user_facing -->|yes| tool_or_solution{Does it bundle
multiple tools?} tool_or_solution -->|yes| solution[level: solution] tool_or_solution -->|no| tool[level: tool] user_facing -->|no| foundation[level: foundation] style block fill:#93c5fd style tool fill:#60a5fa,color:#fff style solution fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff style foundation fill:#bfdbfe style integration fill:#dbeafe ``` ### Examples and anti-examples - **`contacts`** Ă© um `block`. Possui seu Prisma model `Contact`, tem CRUD, e Ă© consumido por tools como `subscription-offering` e `lead-qualification`. - **`subscription-offering`** Ă© uma `tool`. CompĂµe contacts + deals + products para gerenciar receita recorrente. Sem dado prĂłprio. - **`packages`** **nĂŁo** Ă© uma entry. Ă‰ um block-group dentro de `products` (uma coleĂ§ĂŁo curada de products com pricing ou marketing compartilhado). Documentado em `products/feature.yml` sob `block_groups`. - **`i18n`** Ă© uma `foundation`. Usada por toda surface de UI mas sem unidade comercial por si sĂł. - **`google-calendar`** Ă© uma `integration`. Sem dado prĂłprio, apenas uma ponte para a API de calendĂˇrio do Google. - **`finances`** (a category em `.agents/tools/finances/AGENT.md`) **nĂŁo** Ă© uma entry. Ă‰ um agrupamento de runtime. O papel comercial vive no level `solution` quando desenhado. - **`Support Solution`** seria uma `solution` (quando a camada estiver ativa) â€” fazendo bundle de tools orientadas a suporte para uma experiĂŞncia de cliente coerente. Hoje sĂł existiria como um futuro `feature.yml` com `status: deferred`. ### The 4 artifacts per feature Toda feature no HERD Ă© descrita por atĂ© quatro artifacts, ligados pelo `id` e pelo `uid`: 1. **Handbook entry** em `docs/handbook/{level}/{id}/{pt-BR.md, en-US.md}` â€” prosa bilĂ­ngue para humanos. 2. **`feature.yml`** no mesmo diretĂłrio â€” metadata canĂ´nico, a join key. 3. **`SKILL.md`** em `.agents/skills/feature-{level}-{id}/SKILL.md` â€” guia operacional voltado para agentes. Opcional; obrigatĂłrio quando `artifacts.skill: true` no `feature.yml`. 4. **MCP tool** registrada em `mcp/generated/manifest.json` â€” exposta a agentes externos. Opcional; obrigatĂłria quando `artifacts.mcp: true`. No day-1 a camada MCP entrega apenas as tools `search` e `fetch` que indexam o prĂłprio Handbook. Tools MCP por feature (ex: `herd_create_contact`) ficam para uma fase posterior. ### Schema as source of truth O schema do `feature.yml` Ă© definido em TypeScript Zod 4 em `schemas/feature.zod.ts`, importado via o subpath `zod/v4`. O JSON Schema Ă© gerado a partir dele via `npm run gen:schemas` e commitado em `schemas/feature.schema.json` â€” isso dĂˇ autocomplete em IDEs e um validator estĂˇvel para CI. Drift entre os dois Ă© pego pelo CI: `git diff --exit-code schemas/` depois de rodar `gen:schemas` precisa estar limpo. ### CI gates TrĂŞs gates hard-fail bloqueiam merges de PR (introduzidos progressivamente ao longo desta etapa, com enforcement total na Sub-etapa 6): - **Schema + path consistency.** O `feature.yml` parseia contra o schema Zod; o `level` casa com o diretĂłrio; o `uid` casa com `herd..`. - **Cross-reference resolution.** Todos os IDs em `consumes`, `consumed_by`, `parent`, `children`, `related` resolvem para `feature.yml` existentes. Refs danglings conhecidas (durante backfill) sĂŁo listadas explicitamente em `docs/handbook/_meta/.legacy-allowlist.txt`, que o Danger.js impede de crescer. - **Generated artifacts freshness.** Rodar `npm run gen:all` produz zero diff; se uma mudanĂ§a no Handbook nĂŁo foi acompanhada da regeneraĂ§ĂŁo dos artifacts, o CI falha. TrĂŞs warnings soft (comentĂˇrios do Danger.js, nĂŁo bloqueiam merge): - Co-mudanĂ§a bilĂ­ngue: `pt-BR.md` editado sem `en-US.md` (ou vice-versa). - EmpurrĂŁo doc-first: cĂłdigo sob `src/components/`, `src/lib/`, `src/app/admin/` mudou sem nenhuma mudanĂ§a em `docs/handbook/`. - Cobertura de perspectives: `feature.yml.perspectives` lista perspectives cujos H2 headers nĂŁo aparecem em ambos os arquivos de locale. ## Operations Esta entry Ă© **operacional** â€” agentes devem tratĂˇ-la como autoritativa. Cinco instruĂ§Ăµes para qualquer agente (Claude Code, ChatGPT via MCP, Claude Desktop via MCP) usando documentaĂ§ĂŁo do HERD: 1. **Antes de escrever cĂłdigo que cria, modifica, ou deprecia uma feature, localize seu `feature.yml`.** Se nenhum existe e vocĂŞ estĂˇ criando algo novo, rode `npm run gen:feature` (a meta-skill `/new-feature`, introduzida na Sub-etapa 3) primeiro. NĂŁo improvise os quatro artifacts Ă  mĂŁo. 2. **O campo `level` Ă© canĂ´nico.** Quando em dĂşvida sobre se algo Ă© uma tool ou uma foundation, percorra a decision tree na Architecture perspective desta entry. Se ainda estiver incerto, pergunte ao usuĂˇrio antes de classificar. 3. **Cross-references usam UIDs (`herd..`), nĂŁo paths.** UIDs sobrevivem a renomeaĂ§Ăµes; paths quebram. O xrefmap em `docs/handbook/_meta/xrefmap.yml` Ă© a tabela canĂ´nica de traduĂ§ĂŁo UID â†’ path. 4. **NĂŁo edite `mcp/generated/`, `schemas/feature.schema.json`, `docs/handbook/_meta/xrefmap.yml`, ou `public/llms.txt` Ă  mĂŁo.** SĂŁo gerados. Rode o script `npm run gen:*` correspondente, ou `npm run gen:all` para regenerar tudo de uma vez. 5. **O contrato bilĂ­ngue Ă© simĂ©trico.** Quando vocĂŞ muda `pt-BR.md`, mude `en-US.md` no mesmo PR (e vice-versa). Se uma traduĂ§ĂŁo estĂˇ pendente, commite um bloco `` no locale que estĂˇ atrasado e marque o PR com a tag `i18n-followup`. ## Glossary | Term (en-US) | Termo (pt-BR) | Significado | |---|---|---| | block | bloco | Fonte Ăşnica da verdade para um tipo de dado. Possui Prisma models. | | block-group | grupo de bloco | ColeĂ§ĂŁo curada intra-block (ex: packages dentro de products). NĂŁo Ă© entry prĂłpria. | | corporate-network | rede corporativa | A plataforma HERD inteira por cliente. Topo da pirĂ˘mide. | | feature.yml | feature.yml | Arquivo de metadata canĂ´nico por feature. A join key entre os quatro artifacts. | | foundation | fundaĂ§ĂŁo | Infraestrutura compartilhada consumida por outros levels (i18n, auth, ledger, etc.). | | Handbook | Handbook | Sistema de documentaĂ§ĂŁo do HERD. Esta entry documenta ele. | | integration | integraĂ§ĂŁo | ConexĂŁo com um sistema externo. Sem dado prĂłprio. | | level | nĂ­vel | Um dos seis valores: corporate-network, solution, tool, block, foundation, integration. | | MCP | MCP | Model Context Protocol. Como agentes externos (ChatGPT, Claude Desktop) consomem docs do HERD. | | perspective | perspectiva | Uma das seis seĂ§Ăµes de uma entry de Handbook: Business, Product, Architecture, Operations, Glossary, Changelog. | | SKILL.md | SKILL.md | Guia operacional voltado para agentes. Formato definido por agentskills.io. | | solution | soluĂ§ĂŁo | Bundle curado de tools para um resultado de negĂłcio. Atualmente deferred. | | technical_category | categoria tĂ©cnica | Uma de trĂŞs: block, tool, foundation. Corta os commercial levels. | | tool | ferramenta | ComposiĂ§ĂŁo cross-block com um objetivo de negĂłcio. | | uid | uid | Identificador estĂˇvel no formato `herd..`. | | xrefmap | xrefmap | Tabela gerada de traduĂ§ĂŁo UID â†’ path. Lookup canĂ´nico para cross-references. | ## Changelog - **2026-05-01** â€” PublicaĂ§ĂŁo inicial. Etapa Handbook foundation + first entries. Estabelece 6 commercial levels (corporate-network, solution, tool, block, foundation, integration), 3 technical categories (block, tool, foundation), 4 artifacts por feature (Handbook, feature.yml, SKILL.md, MCP), Zod 4 (via subpath `zod/v4`) como schema source-of-truth, doc-first como workflow, e CI gates (3 hard-fail + 3 soft warning).
entries.body_en_US:
--- title: Handbook description: HERD's documentation system â€” 4 artifacts, 6 levels, doc-first. locale: en-US uid: herd.foundation.handbook --- > **For AI agents:** This Markdown file is the canonical form of this entry. Use `Accept: text/markdown` or append `.md` to the URL to avoid HTML rendering. # Handbook The Handbook is HERD's documentation system. Every feature in the platform â€” block, tool, foundation, or integration â€” is described here using a consistent template, so that humans, internal AI agents (Claude Code), and external AI agents (ChatGPT, Claude Desktop via MCP) can all build a correct mental model of HERD without reading source code. This entry documents the Handbook itself: what the levels mean, how `feature.yml` works, how to read or write a Handbook entry, and how the system stays consistent under change. ## Business The Handbook exists because HERD's product surface is large and growing. As blocks, tools, foundations, and integrations multiply, knowing what each one is, why it exists, who it serves, and how it relates to the others becomes the central onboarding bottleneck â€” both for humans joining the team and for agents asked to do work in the codebase. The cost of bad documentation in an AI-collaborated codebase is materially higher than in a traditional one. When an agent doesn't know what a feature is, it doesn't ask â€” it guesses. Guesses produce code that compiles, runs, and quietly does the wrong thing. The Handbook eliminates the conditions under which an agent guesses by providing a single, canonical, machine-readable description of every feature it might touch. For HERD's clients, the Handbook is invisible â€” but its effects are not. Faster, more consistent feature delivery; fewer regressions caused by misunderstood semantics; agent-driven workflows (via MCP) that work because the agent has access to the same documentation a senior engineer would consult. The Handbook is also the substrate for HERD's positioning as a Market Network Platform. When the platform federates across corporate networks, the Handbook is the contract that lets agents in one network discover and reason about capabilities in another without bespoke integration work. ## Product The Handbook surfaces in three places. For humans on the team, it lives at `/admin/handbook` inside HERD itself, with a left sidebar grouping entries by level (Foundations, Blocks, Tools, Solutions, Integrations, Corporate Network), and a sticky table of contents on each entry showing the six perspectives. UI-side rendering is delivered in a later etapa; the filesystem source is what exists today. For Claude Code working in the repo, it lives at `docs/handbook/{level}/{feature-id}/{pt-BR,en-US}.md` â€” read directly from the filesystem, paired with the relevant `SKILL.md` packages in `.agents/skills/`. For external agents (ChatGPT, Claude Desktop) connecting via MCP, it surfaces through two tools: `search(query)` returns matching feature UIDs; `fetch(id)` returns the full Markdown content of an entry plus its metadata graph (consumes, consumed_by, related). A user reading the Handbook in HERD's admin sees: an entry's title, status badge (active / draft / deprecated / archived / deferred), version, last-updated date, and the six perspectives as collapsible sections. Cross-references render as clickable links to other entries. ## Architecture ### The 6 commercial levels HERD's product is organized as a pyramid of six commercial levels. Every documentable feature in HERD belongs to exactly one level. The levels, from top to bottom, are: ```mermaid flowchart TD CN[Corporate Network] --> SOL[Solution] SOL --> TOOL[Tool] TOOL --> BLOCK[Block] BLOCK --> FDN[Foundation] FDN --> INT[Integration] style CN fill:#1e40af,color:#fff style SOL fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff style TOOL fill:#60a5fa,color:#000 style BLOCK fill:#93c5fd,color:#000 style FDN fill:#bfdbfe,color:#000 style INT fill:#dbeafe,color:#000 ``` - **Corporate Network** â€” the entire HERD platform as it appears to a single client company. The top tier. (When the Market Network layer lands, it will sit above this.) - **Solution** â€” a curated bundle of tools sold or framed for a specific business outcome. Examples: Support, Pre-sales, Sales, Marketing. *Currently deferred â€” the schema accepts the level but no entries exist day-1; returns when the Solution layer is designed.* - **Tool** â€” a cross-block composition with a specific business objective. A tool reads from and writes to multiple blocks, applies business logic, and exposes a focused user-facing or agent-facing capability. Example: `subscription-offering` (consumes contacts + deals + products to manage recurring revenue). - **Block** â€” a single source of truth for a data type. A block has its own Prisma models, CRUD endpoints, lifecycle, RLS policies, and (usually) a manifest in `src/lib/blocks/blocks/{name}.block.ts`. Examples: `contacts`, `meetings`, `deals`, `products`. - **Foundation** â€” shared infrastructure that supports the levels above. Foundations have no commercial unit on their own; they are necessary preconditions for blocks, tools, and solutions to function. Examples: `i18n`, `domain-events`, `auth`, `permissions`, `audit`, `ledger`, `handbook` (this entry), `knowledge`, `agents`, `routines`. - **Integration** â€” a connection to an external system. Integrations have no own data; they are bridges. Examples: `google-calendar`, `slack`, `stripe`. ### The 3 technical categories Cutting across the commercial levels, HERD code falls into three technical categories used in the `feature.yml.technical_category` field: - `block` â€” owns its own data (Prisma models, CRUD). - `tool` â€” composes data owned by blocks. - `foundation` â€” shared infrastructure consumed by everyone. Note: `block-group` is **not** a level and **not** a technical category. Block-groups are intra-block agrupamentos â€” for example, "packages" as a curated group of products inside the products block, or a curated set of meetings filtered by some criterion. They are documented inside the parent block's Architecture perspective (in the `block_groups` field of the parent's `feature.yml`), not as separate entries. Note: `category` (Finances, Legal, Marketing, Sales, Operations) is **not** a level. Categories are runtime agrupamentos that the orchestrator uses to route tool calls. They have agents in `.agents/tools/{category}/AGENT.md` but no Handbook entries â€” the commercial role they would play is subsumed by Solution. ### Decision tree: classifying a new feature When introducing a new feature into HERD, walk the tree below to classify it. The level determines the directory path, the artifacts required, and the audience that will consume the entry. ```mermaid flowchart TD start{Does it own its own
Prisma data?} start -->|yes| block[level: block] start -->|no| owns_external{Does it connect
to an external
system?} owns_external -->|yes| integration[level: integration] owns_external -->|no| user_facing{Is it user-
facing as a
capability?} user_facing -->|yes| tool_or_solution{Does it bundle
multiple tools?} tool_or_solution -->|yes| solution[level: solution] tool_or_solution -->|no| tool[level: tool] user_facing -->|no| foundation[level: foundation] style block fill:#93c5fd style tool fill:#60a5fa,color:#fff style solution fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff style foundation fill:#bfdbfe style integration fill:#dbeafe ``` ### Examples and anti-examples - **`contacts`** is a `block`. It owns its `Contact` Prisma model, has CRUD, and is consumed by tools like `subscription-offering` and `lead-qualification`. - **`subscription-offering`** is a `tool`. It composes contacts + deals + products to manage recurring revenue. No own data. - **`packages`** is **not** an entry. It is a block-group inside `products` (a curated collection of products with shared pricing or marketing). Documented in `products/feature.yml` under `block_groups`. - **`i18n`** is a `foundation`. Used by every UI surface but has no commercial unit on its own. - **`google-calendar`** is an `integration`. No own data, only a bridge to Google's calendar API. - **`finances`** (the category in `.agents/tools/finances/AGENT.md`) is **not** an entry. It is a runtime agrupamento. The commercial role lives at `solution` level when designed. - **`Support Solution`** would be a `solution` (when the layer is active) â€” bundling support-oriented tools for a coherent customer experience. Today it would only exist as a future `feature.yml` with `status: deferred`. ### The 4 artifacts per feature Every feature in HERD is described by up to four artifacts, joined by the `id` and `uid`: 1. **Handbook entry** at `docs/handbook/{level}/{id}/{pt-BR.md, en-US.md}` â€” bilingual prose for humans. 2. **`feature.yml`** at the same directory â€” canonical metadata, the join key. 3. **`SKILL.md`** at `.agents/skills/feature-{level}-{id}/SKILL.md` â€” agent-facing operational guide. Optional; required when `artifacts.skill: true` in `feature.yml`. 4. **MCP tool** registered in `mcp/generated/manifest.json` â€” exposed to external agents. Optional; required when `artifacts.mcp: true`. Day-1 the MCP layer ships only `search` and `fetch` tools that index the Handbook itself. Per-feature MCP tools (e.g., `herd_create_contact`) are deferred to a later phase. ### Schema as source of truth The `feature.yml` schema is defined in TypeScript Zod 4 at `schemas/feature.zod.ts`, imported via the `zod/v4` subpath. JSON Schema is generated from it via `npm run gen:schemas` and committed to `schemas/feature.schema.json` â€” this gives IDEs autocomplete and CI a stable validator. Drift between the two is caught by CI: `git diff --exit-code schemas/` after running `gen:schemas` must be clean. ### CI gates Three hard-fail gates block PR merges (introduced progressively across this etapa, with full enforcement in Sub-etapa 6): - **Schema + path consistency.** `feature.yml` parses against the Zod schema; `level` matches directory; `uid` matches `herd..`. - **Cross-reference resolution.** All `consumes`, `consumed_by`, `parent`, `children`, `related` IDs resolve to existing `feature.yml` files. Known dangling refs (during backfill) are explicitly listed in `docs/handbook/_meta/.legacy-allowlist.txt`, which Danger.js prevents from growing. - **Generated artifacts freshness.** Running `npm run gen:all` produces no diff; if a Handbook change wasn't accompanied by the regenerated artifacts, CI fails. Three soft warnings (Danger.js comments, don't block merges): - Bilingual co-change: `pt-BR.md` edited without `en-US.md` (or vice versa). - Doc-first nudge: source under `src/components/`, `src/lib/`, `src/app/admin/` changed without any `docs/handbook/` change. - Perspective coverage: `feature.yml.perspectives` lists perspectives whose H2 headers don't all appear in both locale files. ## Operations This entry is **operational** â€” agents should treat it as authoritative. Five instructions for any agent (Claude Code, ChatGPT via MCP, Claude Desktop via MCP) using HERD documentation: 1. **Before writing code that creates, modifies, or deprecates a feature, locate its `feature.yml`.** If none exists and you are creating something new, run `npm run gen:feature` (the `/new-feature` meta-skill, introduced in Sub-etapa 3) first. Do not improvise the four artifacts by hand. 2. **The `level` field is canonical.** When in doubt about whether something is a tool or a foundation, walk the decision tree in this entry's Architecture perspective. If still unclear, ask the user before classifying. 3. **Cross-references use UIDs (`herd..`), not file paths.** UIDs survive renames; file paths break. The xrefmap at `docs/handbook/_meta/xrefmap.yml` is the canonical UID â†’ path translation table. 4. **Do not edit `mcp/generated/`, `schemas/feature.schema.json`, `docs/handbook/_meta/xrefmap.yml`, or `public/llms.txt` by hand.** They are generated. Run the corresponding `npm run gen:*` script, or `npm run gen:all` to regenerate everything at once. 5. **The bilingual contract is symmetric.** When you change `pt-BR.md`, change `en-US.md` in the same PR (and vice versa). If a translation is pending, commit a `` block in the locale that lags and tag the PR `i18n-followup`. ## Glossary | Term (en-US) | Termo (pt-BR) | Meaning | |---|---|---| | block | bloco | Single source of truth for a data type. Owns Prisma models. | | block-group | grupo de bloco | Intra-block curated collection (e.g., packages in products). Not its own entry. | | corporate-network | rede corporativa | The entire HERD platform per client. Top of the pyramid. | | feature.yml | feature.yml | Canonical metadata file per feature. This entry documents it. | | integration | integraĂ§ĂŁo | Connection to an external system. No own data. | | level | nĂ­vel | One of the six values: corporate-network, solution, tool, block, foundation, integration. | | MCP | MCP | Model Context Protocol. How external agents (ChatGPT, Claude Desktop) consume HERD docs. | | perspective | perspectiva | One of the six sections of a Handbook entry: Business, Product, Architecture, Operations, Glossary, Changelog. | | SKILL.md | SKILL.md | Agent-facing operational guide. Canonical lookup for cross-references. | ## Changelog - **2026-05-01** â€” Initial publication. Etapa Handbook foundation + first entries. Establishes 6 commercial levels (corporate-network, solution, tool, block, foundation, integration), 3 technical categories (block, tool, foundation), 4 artifacts per feature (Handbook, feature.yml, SKILL.md, MCP), Zod 4 (via `zod/v4` subpath) as schema source-of-truth, doc-first as workflow, and CI gates (3 hard-fail + 3 soft warning).
